Pedagogical Approaches

Secondary Science — Teacher Resource
Download PBL.docx
  • Problem-Based Learning (PBL) is a pedagogical approach where students learn through solving a complex real-world problem that has no single correct answer. Students work collaboratively to identify what they need to learn, engage in self-directed learning, apply that new knowledge to the problem, and then reflect on both what they learned and how effective their strategies were. The teacher acts as a facilitator rather than a knowledge provider. The various stages of PBL also provide opportunities to develop the Ways of Thinking and Doing (WOTD) in students.
Key Question:

How should Singapore manage the impact of acidic rainfall on infrastructure and heritage building conservation?

Singapore's heritage buildings are more than old structures; they are tangible records of the nation's history, cultural identity and collective memory. Landmarks such as churches, monuments, memorials, and colonial-era buildings connect present-day Singaporeans to their past. Once damaged, many of their original features and architectural details cannot be fully restored.

In this Problem-Based Learning experience, students explore the challenge of protecting heritage buildings from the long-term effects of acidic rainfall. Although Singapore does not experience acid rain at the same severity as some heavily industrialised regions, air pollutants can dissolve in rainwater to form acids. Over time, acidic rainfall can react with building materials such as limestone and marble, which contain calcium carbonate. These reactions gradually erode surfaces, weaken structures, and remove intricate carvings and inscriptions that hold historical significance.

Using heritage conservation as the context helps students develop an affective connection to the issue. Rather than learning acid-base chemistry as an abstract topic, students engage with a real-world problem that has local relevance and social significance. They consider how scientific knowledge can contribute to preserving places that matter to Singapore and future generations.

The context also presents a hopeful and action-oriented perspective. While the effects of acidic rainfall can accumulate over long periods, scientific understanding enables early detection, preventive maintenance, material selection, and conservation strategies that can reduce damage. Students investigate the chemistry behind acidic rainfall, analyse evidence on its impact on different materials, and evaluate possible mitigation measures.

  • This LE is situated within a learning sequence that includes all the phases of PBL.
  • In this learning sequence, students first encounter an authentic problem on acidic rainfall on Singapore’s infrastructure and heritage building conservation.
  • Students identify relevant facts and knowledge gaps, generate hypotheses about how acidic rainfall affects carbonate-based materials, conduct a practical investigation using acidic solutions and marble chips or limestone, apply their new knowledge to interpret evidence, and finally propose evidence-informed conservation actions.
PhaseTeachers' RoleStudents' RoleAlignment of PBL to the 3 aims
Problem Scenario
  • Present the problem: “How should Singapore manage the impact of acidic rainfall on infrastructure and heritage building conservation?”
  • Tech Use curated virtual tours to build an affective connection to Singapore's heritage buildings (e.g. Neil Road Townhouse). Students are drawn into the lived history of these buildings: tracing their origins, who once inhabited them, and the stories behind their architecture and design. Students encounter heritage through human stories and prompting them to ask their own questions and reflect on why these buildings and the memories embedded in them are worth conserving.
  • Tech Use images or videos of weathered buildings, students can also identify the materials used to build the house, and then in class have an investigation about the material's reactivity with acid.
  • Teachers can also bring students on a learning journey to visit these places of interest.
  • Ask questions to clarify the problem.
  • Identify why the issue matters scientifically, historically and socially.
  • Share initial ideas about how acidic rainfall may affect buildings.
  • Explain why heritage conservation matters.
AppreciateStudents see the relevance of science in protecting infrastructure and heritage buildings in Singapore. They recognise that science can help address issues that affect society, culture and the environment.
Identify Facts
to formulate and analyse the problem
  • Guide students to identify key facts from the scenario.
  • Use prompts such as:
    • “What do we observe?”
    • “What do we already know?”
    • “What scientific ideas may be relevant?”
    • “What information do we need before proposing a solution?”
  • Tech Use videos, animations or interactives to introduce acid rain, weathering and effects on carbonate-based materials.
  • Tech Use a teacher demonstration or video showing acid reacting with marble chips to introduce the phenomenon.
  • Tech Use platforms such as Google Docs, Padlet or SLS discussion boards for students to co-construct facts, observations and questions.
  • Identify observable facts from images, videos and demonstrations.
  • Recap prior knowledge about acids, pH, chemical reactions, carbonates and environmental conditions.
  • Generate questions about what they need to investigate further.
UnderstandStudents connect real-world observations to scientific ideas such as acids and bases, pH, chemical reactions and material properties.
Generate Hypotheses
  • Facilitate students in proposing possible relationships between the acidity of rainfall, the properties of building materials, and the rate or extent of material degradation.
  • Use prompts such as:
    • "How might increasing acidity affect the reaction between acidic rainfall and marble or limestone?"
    • "Which building materials might be more vulnerable to acidic rainfall, and why?"
    • "What evidence would support or challenge your explanation?
  • Tech Use simulations, animations, or interactive models with embedded feedback to help students visualise particle interactions and explore initial predictions.
  • Generate hypotheses about how acidic rainfall may affect different building materials.
  • Predict how factors such as pH, acid concentration, and material composition may influence the rate and extent of damage.
  • Formulate investigable questions and identify the evidence required to test their hypotheses.
  • Refine their hypotheses based on initial observations, simulations, or available information.
UnderstandStudents develop conceptual understanding by proposing and refining explanations for the relationships between acidity, material properties, and chemical weathering.
UnderstandStudents engage in the practices of science by formulating investigable questions, developing hypotheses, and identifying evidence needed to evaluate competing explanations.
Identify Knowledge Deficiencies
for self-directed learning
  • Facilitate students in identifying what they need to know to solve the problem.
  • Guide students to consider knowledge needed to solve the problem, including:
    • pH and acidity
    • acid-carbonate reactions
    • factors affecting rate of reaction
    • properties of materials
    • heritage buildings conservation approaches
  • Tech Use digital reflection journals or portfolios to support reflection. The following prompts can be used to guide students:
    • “What do I understand?”
    • “What am I unsure about?”
    • “What do I need to find out next?”
  • Identify knowledge gaps and learning needs.
  • Formulate research questions
  • Conduct guided research using curated sources.
  • Record questions and learning points in a reflection journal or group learning log.
Take ActionStudents take ownership of their learning by identifying and addressing knowledge gaps needed to solve a real-world problem.
Apply New Knowledge
to evaluate their hypotheses in light of what they have learnt
  • Support students in carrying out a structured practical investigation using solutions of different pH and building materials (e.g., carbonate, other possible materials proposed for the solution)
  • Facilitate safe collection of quantitative and qualitative evidence
  • Tech Use digital probes, sensors, balances, data loggers, spreadsheets, or AI-supported tools for data collection and analysis.
  • Guide students to connect experimental findings to the broader conservation efforts
  • Design or conduct investigations to gather evidence.
  • Collect, record, and analyse data.
  • Identify patterns and trends.
  • Relate findings to the effects of acidic rainfall on heritage buildings and infrastructure.
  • Evaluate the strengths and limitations of their evidence.
UnderstandStudents deepen conceptual understanding of acids, pH, reaction rates, and carbonate reactions through evidence-based investigation.
UnderstandStudents engage in scientific inquiry and construct explanations using data.
Consolidation and Solution Development
  • Guide students to construct evidence-based explanations using a Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) framework.
  • Help students explain how acidic rainfall affects carbonate-based building materials.
  • Facilitate reflection on how the practical investigation informs understanding of the real-world problem.
  • Challenge students to evaluate possible conservation strategies and justify recommendations using scientific evidence.
  • Provide the action task: Select a heritage building in Singapore, identify potential vulnerabilities to acidic rainfall, and propose a conservation plan.
  • Construct and communicate evidence-based explanations.
  • Connect practical findings to the original problem context.
  • Reflect on the reliability and limitations of their evidence.
  • Develop, justify, and communicate a conservation plan using evidence from investigations and research.
  • Evaluate the strengths and limitations of different conservation approaches.
UnderstandStudents consolidate scientific understanding by linking evidence to explanations about acid-carbonate reactions and material degradation.
AppreciateStudents recognise how scientific evidence informs decisions about protecting infrastructure and heritage assets.
Take ActionStudents apply scientific understanding to propose and justify evidence-informed solutions to a real-world challenge.

Problem Scenario

Teachers' Role
  • Present the problem: “How should Singapore manage the impact of acidic rainfall on infrastructure and heritage building conservation?”
  • Tech Use curated virtual tours to build an affective connection to Singapore's heritage buildings (e.g. Neil Road Townhouse). Students are drawn into the lived history of these buildings: tracing their origins, who once inhabited them, and the stories behind their architecture and design. Students encounter heritage through human stories and prompting them to ask their own questions and reflect on why these buildings and the memories embedded in them are worth conserving.
  • Tech Use images or videos of weathered buildings, students can also identify the materials used to build the house, and then in class have an investigation about the material's reactivity with acid.
  • Teachers can also bring students on a learning journey to visit these places of interest.
Students' Role
  • Ask questions to clarify the problem.
  • Identify why the issue matters scientifically, historically and socially.
  • Share initial ideas about how acidic rainfall may affect buildings.
  • Explain why heritage conservation matters.
AppreciateStudents see the relevance of science in protecting infrastructure and heritage buildings in Singapore. They recognise that science can help address issues that affect society, culture and the environment.

Identify Facts

to formulate and analyse the problem
Teachers' Role
  • Guide students to identify key facts from the scenario.
  • Use prompts such as:
    • “What do we observe?”
    • “What do we already know?”
    • “What scientific ideas may be relevant?”
    • “What information do we need before proposing a solution?”
  • Tech Use videos, animations or interactives to introduce acid rain, weathering and effects on carbonate-based materials.
  • Tech Use a teacher demonstration or video showing acid reacting with marble chips to introduce the phenomenon.
  • Tech Use platforms such as Google Docs, Padlet or SLS discussion boards for students to co-construct facts, observations and questions.
Students' Role
  • Identify observable facts from images, videos and demonstrations.
  • Recap prior knowledge about acids, pH, chemical reactions, carbonates and environmental conditions.
  • Generate questions about what they need to investigate further.
UnderstandStudents connect real-world observations to scientific ideas such as acids and bases, pH, chemical reactions and material properties.

Teachers' Role
  • Facilitate students in proposing possible relationships between the acidity of rainfall, the properties of building materials, and the rate or extent of material degradation.
  • Use prompts such as:
    • "How might increasing acidity affect the reaction between acidic rainfall and marble or limestone?"
    • "Which building materials might be more vulnerable to acidic rainfall, and why?"
    • "What evidence would support or challenge your explanation?
  • Tech Use simulations, animations, or interactive models with embedded feedback to help students visualise particle interactions and explore initial predictions.
Students' Role
  • Generate hypotheses about how acidic rainfall may affect different building materials.
  • Predict how factors such as pH, acid concentration, and material composition may influence the rate and extent of damage.
  • Formulate investigable questions and identify the evidence required to test their hypotheses.
  • Refine their hypotheses based on initial observations, simulations, or available information.
UnderstandStudents develop conceptual understanding by proposing and refining explanations for the relationships between acidity, material properties, and chemical weathering.
UnderstandStudents engage in the practices of science by formulating investigable questions, developing hypotheses, and identifying evidence needed to evaluate competing explanations.

  • Guide students to consider knowledge needed to solve the problem, including:
    • pH and acidity

Students' Role
  • Identify knowledge gaps and learning needs.
  • Formulate research questions
  • Conduct guided research using curated sources.

to evaluate their hypotheses in light of what they have learnt
Teachers' Role
  • Support students in carrying out a structured practical investigation using solutions of different pH and building materials (e.g., carbonate, other possible materials proposed for the solution)
  • Facilitate safe collection of quantitative and qualitative evidence
  • Tech Use digital probes, sensors, balances, data loggers, spreadsheets, or AI-supported tools for data collection and analysis.
  • Guide students to connect experimental findings to the broader conservation efforts
Students' Role
  • Design or conduct investigations to gather evidence.
  • Collect, record, and analyse data.
  • Identify patterns and trends.

  • Evaluate the strengths and limitations of their evidence.

Consolidation and Solution Development

Teachers' Role
  • Guide students to construct evidence-based explanations using a Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) framework.
  • Help students explain how acidic rainfall affects carbonate-based building materials.
  • Facilitate reflection on how the practical investigation informs understanding of the real-world problem.
  • Challenge students to evaluate possible conservation strategies and justify recommendations using scientific evidence.
  • Provide the action task: Select a heritage building in Singapore, identify potential vulnerabilities to acidic rainfall, and propose a conservation plan.

  • Construct and communicate evidence-based explanations.
  • Connect practical findings to the original problem context.
  • Reflect on the reliability and limitations of their evidence.
  • Develop, justify, and communicate a conservation plan using evidence from investigations and research.
  • Evaluate the strengths and limitations of different conservation approaches.
UnderstandStudents consolidate scientific understanding by linking evidence to explanations about acid-carbonate reactions and material degradation.
AppreciateStudents recognise how scientific evidence informs decisions about protecting infrastructure and heritage assets.
Take ActionStudents apply scientific understanding to propose and justify evidence-informed solutions to a real-world challenge.
  • Problem-based learning can be differentiated for students of different readiness.
  • The essence of PBL is preserved in all differentiation, where students engage in sense-making the authentic problem, identifying learning needs, gathering and evaluating evidence, constructing explanations and proposing evidence-information solutions.
PBL phaseFor lower readiness studentsFor higher readiness students
Problem Scenario
  • Use guiding questions to help students identify the key problem, stakeholders, and possible impacts of acidic rainfall.
  • Provide structured prompts to help students connect the issue to heritage conservation and everyday life.
  • Introduce key vocabulary and concepts before engaging with the scenario.
  • Encourage students to independently identify relevant scientific, environmental, social, economic, and cultural dimensions of the problem.
  • Invite students to formulate their own problem statements and identify stakeholders.
Identify Facts
to formulate and analyse the problem
  • Provide graphic organisers to categorise facts, observations, assumptions, and questions.
  • Use curated resources and targeted prompts to support information gathering.
  • Guide students in identifying relevant scientific concepts and information needed to address the problem.
  • Encourage students to independently determine what information is relevant and identify additional information required.
  • Require students to evaluate the credibility and relevance of different information sources.
Generate Hypotheses
  • Provide hypothesis stems, guiding questions, and examples to support causal reasoning.
  • Focus students on a smaller number of variables when considering possible explanations.
  • Encourage students to generate multiple hypotheses and consider interactions between variables.
  • Require students to justify hypotheses using scientific principles and prior knowledge.
Identify Knowledge Deficiencies
for self-directed learning
  • Use reflection prompts to help students identify gaps in their understanding.
  • Provide curated resources at an appropriate level to support knowledge building.
  • Encourage students to independently identify knowledge gaps, formulate research questions, and locate relevant sources.
  • Require students to synthesise information from multiple sources.
Apply New Knowledge
to evaluate their hypotheses in light of what they have learnt
  • Provide structured investigation procedures, observation prompts, data tables, and graphing templates.
  • Reduce the number of variables students need to manage while ensuring they collect and interpret evidence relevant to the problem.
  • Support students in connecting investigation findings to the effects of acidic rainfall on heritage buildings.
  • Provide greater autonomy in planning or modifying investigations
  • Allow students to investigate multiple variables (e.g., pH, concentration, exposure time, material type) and justify methodological choices.
  • Require students to evaluate the quality and limitations of their evidence.
Consolidation and Solution Development
  • Provide Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) scaffolds, sentence starters, and guiding questions to support explanation building.
  • Support students in linking evidence to scientific concepts and the original problem.
  • Provide a set of possible conservation strategies for students to evaluate and justify using evidence from their investigation and research.
  • Use structured planning templates to support decision-making.
  • Require students to independently construct explanations and evaluate alternative interpretations.
  • Challenge students to critique the reliability, validity, and limitations of their evidence and reasoning.
  • Require students to generate, compare, and defend their own conservation strategies.
  • Challenge students to evaluate trade-offs such as cost, feasibility, effectiveness, sustainability, aesthetics, and heritage value.
  • Require students to justify recommendations using multiple sources of evidence.
Download IBL.docx
  • This LE exemplifies the five essential features of inquiry. Not all LEs are expected to incorporate all five features and may focus on selected aspects of the inquiry process.
Essential Features of Science as InquiryTeacher's RoleStudents' RoleAlignment of IBL to the 3 aims
Question
Learners are engaged by scientifically oriented questions
  • Demonstrates the reaction between sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid to create curiosity around an observable phenomenon
  • Tech A video demonstrating the reaction may be used. Captions and pauses may be included to guide students’ thinking.
  • Prompts students to observe and generate investigable questions related to factors affecting the rate of reaction.
  • Scaffolds students in refining questions to ensure that they are measurable, feasible, and scientifically investigable.
    • “What measurements could we make to compare the rate of reactions?”
    • “What factors might influence how quickly the solution turns cloudy?”
    • “Which factor would you like to investigate?”
  • Tech Supports question generation through AI-enabled chatbot, where appropriate
  • Observe the reaction and identify variables that may affect the rate of reaction (e.g., concentration, temperature, surface area of reactants).
  • Formulate and refine an inquiry question related to reaction rate
  • Develop hypotheses and justify why a chosen variable may influence the reaction rate based on prior knowledge
  • Tech Use AI-enabled chatbot in generating and refining the investigable questions.
UnderstandStudents' prior knowledge is activated. Students are engaged in formulating investigable questions and hypotheses, mirroring authentic scientific practices.
AppreciateIBL can be grounded in real-world problems and anchor phenomena, making it directly relevant to students' lives. For example: (a) What causes everyday metal objects to rust? (b) How can we design barriers to prevent rusting? Open-ended questions encourage students to connect scientific concepts to their real-world applications.
Evidence
Learners give priority to evidence
  • Facilitates the planning of investigations by providing feedback on variables, controls, methods, and safety considerations.
  • Tech Supports students in using digital tools for planning, collaboration, and data collection.
  • Design and conduct investigations to examine the effect of selected variables on reaction rate.
  • Collect, organise, and analyse data systematically.
  • Use graphs and data trends to identify patterns and compare findings across groups.
  • Tech Document their investigation plans digitally to enable teacher feedback and monitoring.
  • Tech Collect, organise, and share data collaboratively using shared spreadsheets or collaborative platforms.
  • Tech Analyse their data by plotting graphs on spreadsheets, identifying trends, and comparing findings across groups investigating different variables.
UnderstandStudents engage in evidence-based reasoning, data collection and analysis which are core science practices that deepen understanding of disciplinary concepts.
Explanations
Learners formulate explanations from evidence
  • Guides students in connecting experimental findings to collision theory.
  • Tech Supports students’ use of simulations and visualisations to deepen conceptual understanding and explain observed trends.
  • Use experimental evidence to explain observed trends in reaction rates.
  • Tech Use simulations and animations to develop particle-level explanations for their findings
  • Refine explanations by integrating evidence from experiments and models.
UnderstandStudents' conceptual understanding is deepened by linking observations to scientific theories and models.
Take ActionThe safe learning environments and scaffolding inherent in IBL encourage students to take intellectual risks, persist through uncertainty, and develop the curiosity and resilience needed for lifelong scientific inquiry. This transforms students from passive recipients of scientific knowledge into active contributors who can innovate and create positive impact for society.
Connections / Evaluation
Learners evaluate their explanations in light of alternative explanations particularly those reflecting scientific understandings
  • Facilitates discussions that compare different findings and surface contrasting explanations
  • Tech Uses AI-enabled tools to summarise discussion, identify common patterns or surface contrasting explanations
  • Provides feedback on the scientific validity of students’ reasoning.
  • Critically examine how well simulations and models explain experimental observations.
  • Compare findings across groups investigating different variables.
  • Evaluate explanations against evidence and accepted scientific understanding (e.g., collision theory).
UnderstandStudents develop critical thinking and deeper understanding through the evaluation of competing explanations.
AppreciateStudents appreciate the nature of science through the process of evaluation and cultivate values such as objectivity, integrity, open-mindedness and respect for evidence.
Communication
Learners communicate and justify their proposed explanations
  • Facilitates whole-class knowledge building by synthesising findings across groups.
  • Tech Use AI-enabled tools to synthesise findings across groups
  • Provides feedback on the quality of explanations and scientific arguments.
  • Communicate findings using evidence-based arguments.
  • Justify conclusions on how different variables affect the rate of reaction using experimental data and scientific concepts.
  • Engage in peer critique and collaborative knowledge construction.
  • Co-construct class explanations about factors affecting reaction rates.
UnderstandAs students communicate findings and engage in peer critique, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence.
AppreciateThrough collaboration and discussion, students move beyond abstract knowledge and begin to see science as a meaningful driver of positive change.
Take ActionThrough collaborative investigations, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings.

Question

Learners are engaged by scientifically oriented questions
Teacher's Role
  • Demonstrates the reaction between sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid to create curiosity around an observable phenomenon
  • Tech A video demonstrating the reaction may be used. Captions and pauses may be included to guide students’ thinking.
  • Prompts students to observe and generate investigable questions related to factors affecting the rate of reaction.
  • Scaffolds students in refining questions to ensure that they are measurable, feasible, and scientifically investigable.
    • “What measurements could we make to compare the rate of reactions?”
    • “What factors might influence how quickly the solution turns cloudy?”
    • “Which factor would you like to investigate?”
  • Tech Supports question generation through AI-enabled chatbot, where appropriate
Students' Role
  • Observe the reaction and identify variables that may affect the rate of reaction (e.g., concentration, temperature, surface area of reactants).
  • Formulate and refine an inquiry question related to reaction rate
  • Develop hypotheses and justify why a chosen variable may influence the reaction rate based on prior knowledge
  • Tech Use AI-enabled chatbot in generating and refining the investigable questions.
UnderstandStudents' prior knowledge is activated. Students are engaged in formulating investigable questions and hypotheses, mirroring authentic scientific practices.
AppreciateIBL can be grounded in real-world problems and anchor phenomena, making it directly relevant to students' lives. For example: (a) What causes everyday metal objects to rust? (b) How can we design barriers to prevent rusting? Open-ended questions encourage students to connect scientific concepts to their real-world applications.

Teacher's Role
  • Facilitates the planning of investigations by providing feedback on variables, controls, methods, and safety considerations.
  • Tech Supports students in using digital tools for planning, collaboration, and data collection.
Students' Role
  • Design and conduct investigations to examine the effect of selected variables on reaction rate.
  • Collect, organise, and analyse data systematically.
  • Use graphs and data trends to identify patterns and compare findings across groups.
  • Tech Document their investigation plans digitally to enable teacher feedback and monitoring.
  • Tech Collect, organise, and share data collaboratively using shared spreadsheets or collaborative platforms.
  • Tech Analyse their data by plotting graphs on spreadsheets, identifying trends, and comparing findings across groups investigating different variables.

Learners formulate explanations from evidence
Teacher's Role
  • Guides students in connecting experimental findings to collision theory.
  • Tech Supports students’ use of simulations and visualisations to deepen conceptual understanding and explain observed trends.
Students' Role
  • Use experimental evidence to explain observed trends in reaction rates.
  • Tech Use simulations and animations to develop particle-level explanations for their findings
  • Refine explanations by integrating evidence from experiments and models.
UnderstandStudents' conceptual understanding is deepened by linking observations to scientific theories and models.
Take ActionThe safe learning environments and scaffolding inherent in IBL encourage students to take intellectual risks, persist through uncertainty, and develop the curiosity and resilience needed for lifelong scientific inquiry. This transforms students from passive recipients of scientific knowledge into active contributors who can innovate and create positive impact for society.

Connections / Evaluation

Learners evaluate their explanations in light of alternative explanations particularly those reflecting scientific understandings
Teacher's Role
  • Facilitates discussions that compare different findings and surface contrasting explanations
  • Tech Uses AI-enabled tools to summarise discussion, identify common patterns or surface contrasting explanations
  • Provides feedback on the scientific validity of students’ reasoning.
Students' Role
  • Critically examine how well simulations and models explain experimental observations.
  • Compare findings across groups investigating different variables.
  • Evaluate explanations against evidence and accepted scientific understanding (e.g., collision theory).
UnderstandStudents develop critical thinking and deeper understanding through the evaluation of competing explanations.
AppreciateStudents appreciate the nature of science through the process of evaluation and cultivate values such as objectivity, integrity, open-mindedness and respect for evidence.

Communication

Learners communicate and justify their proposed explanations
Teacher's Role
  • Facilitates whole-class knowledge building by synthesising findings across groups.
  • Tech Use AI-enabled tools to synthesise findings across groups
  • Provides feedback on the quality of explanations and scientific arguments.
Students' Role
  • Communicate findings using evidence-based arguments.
  • Justify conclusions on how different variables affect the rate of reaction using experimental data and scientific concepts.
  • Engage in peer critique and collaborative knowledge construction.
  • Co-construct class explanations about factors affecting reaction rates.
UnderstandAs students communicate findings and engage in peer critique, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence.
AppreciateThrough collaboration and discussion, students move beyond abstract knowledge and begin to see science as a meaningful driver of positive change.
Take ActionThrough collaborative investigations, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings.
  • The learning experience described above exemplifies open inquiry, where students exercise a high degree of ownership over the inquiry process. Inquiry-based learning (IBL) can be differentiated according to students’ readiness by varying the extent of teacher guidance and student self-direction across the essential features of inquiry.
  • Regardless of the level of guidance provided, the core intent of IBL remains unchanged. Students engage with authentic questions, gather and use evidence to construct explanations, persevere through uncertainty, and develop curiosity about the natural world.
  • The degree of “openness” in an inquiry is determined by the extent to which students assume responsibility for posing questions, collecting and analysing evidence, constructing and evaluating explanations, and communicating their findings.
  • To develop students’ inquiry capabilities progressively, learning experiences should vary in their level of openness. Across their science learning journey, students should have opportunities to engage in a range of inquiry experiences—from confirmation and structured inquiry to guided and open inquiry—as they build the knowledge, skills, and dispositions needed to inquire independently.
Reference
  • National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. 2000. Inquiry and the National Science Education Standards: A Guide for Teaching and Learning. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/9596.
Download CBL.docx
  • Concept-based learning is about big transferable ideas, such as Disciplinary Ideas, that transcend context, location and time.
  • In CBL, students have opportunities to see connections among disparate facts and arrive at the larger disciplinary ideas.
  • The following questions are characteristic of CBL:
  • What patterns do you notice across these different examples?
  • How does this concept apply in other contexts you know?
  • What big idea connects all these specific cases?
  • How can you transfer this understanding to solve a new problem?
  • This LE is situated within a lesson which includes all the phases of CBL. (Not all lessons need to have all phases.)
  • In this lesson, students first ‘Relate’ to and ‘Acquire’ one concept associated with air resistance.
  • They then ‘Connect’ to other concepts (e.g. resultant force, acceleration) which are taught in previous lessons through a generalization (‘Forces help us understand motion’).
  • In this lesson, they also ‘Transfer’ their learning to other contexts.
  • In a self-directed digital environment, the role traditionally played by the teacher to facilitate Socratic questioning, metacognitive reflection and introducing counter cases for transfer is enabled by EdTech features such as SLS components (e.g. Free Response Questions, Discussion), SALiS (the SLS chatbot), and HTML5 interactives.
  • The ‘Tech’ descriptors illustrate the learning experience when (i) EdTech is used in-class and (ii) designed as an Elective Module (EM).
PhaseTeachers' RoleStudents' RoleAlignment of CBL to the 3 aims
Relate
to students’ prior knowledge and lived experiences
  • Pose a challenge that draws on prior knowledge / lived experience
    • "How can you make a single sheet of paper fall at the same rate as a ream of paper?
  • Tech A ‘Discussion’ could be set up on SLS for students to make their thinking visible in-class. Teacher can then use ‘Data Assistant’ to consolidate their responses and generate a whole-class overview.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, the SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner, probing student reasoning using prompts like "What did you try? / Why did you think that would work? / What did you observe? / Why did you think your method worked / not work?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students in-class.
  • Generate ideas and investigate using the sheet of paper and ream of paper provided.
AppreciateWhen real-world contexts are used to illustrate concepts, students can see the relevance of science.
Acquire
knowledge of single concepts through meaning making
  • Sequence cases and phenomena
    • "What do you predict will happen when we drop a bowling ball and a feather at the same time?"
    • "Now what do you predict will happen in a vacuum?"
  • Tech To make students’ thinking visible, a ‘Discussion’ could sequence the bowling ball and feather videos with prediction prompts between each: "Predict, then observe. What surprised you?" Teacher can then use ‘Data Assistant’ to get a consolidated in-class overview.
  • Tech An HTML5 interactive could allow students to vary mass and surface area and observe the effect on fall rate.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, the SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner using prompts like "What pattern did you notice in the interactive? / Does this match what you saw in the video? / What concept might explain this?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students.
  • Predict, observe, and make meaning
AppreciateWhen real-world contexts are used to illustrate concepts, students can see the relevance of science.
Connect
different concepts
  • (Other related concepts (e.g. weight, free body diagram, acceleration, net force) taught in previous lessons.)
  • Provide the context
    • "Why does the skydiver change his posture during the rescue?"
    • "Can you use a free body diagram to show what is happening to the forces?"
    • "What would help explain motion?"
  • Tech A ‘Discussion’ presents the skydiving video with minimal framing: "Why does the skydiver change posture? Use the interactive if it helps.”
  • Tech A dynamically-linked HTML5 interactive showing multiple representations could be made available but students decide how to use it. Teacher could use the interactive in-class with the overview of responses generated by ‘Data Assistant’.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, SLS chatbot is configured as a Socratic questioner, probing the student's emerging generalisation without confirming it: “What could help explain motion? "You wrote that more air resistance slows the skydiver down. Is that always true?" "What does your free body diagram show about the net force when air resistance equals weight?" "How would you refine your statement to account for that?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students.
  • Generate the relationship question and construct the generalisation with facilitation
Understand Disciplinary IdeasCBL is explicitly designed to develop deep conceptual understanding of Disciplinary Ideas that transcend context. The process of connecting isolated facts to big ideas cultivates curiosity and open-mindedness.
Transfer
learning to novel contexts and the real world
  • Provide the transfer context
    • "What do you notice when the parachute opens?" (skydiver seen to fly upwards)
    • "Can you explain this in your own words using what you have established?"
    • "Where else in the real world might your generalisation apply?"
  • Tech Students could individually replay the video embedded on SLS to observe the motion of the skydiver when the parachute is displayed.
  • Tech Students could also use the above-mentioned HTML5 interactive to evaluate their transfer.
  • Tech SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner, probing the student's explanation and adaptively introducing a further transfer context based on the quality of the response: "What do you notice when the parachute opens? Explain this in your own words using your generalisation. / You said the skydiver moves upwards. Does your generalisation predict this? Walk me through it. / Your explanation holds here. Can you think of another real-world situation where the same generalisation applies?"
  • Provide additional contexts through worksheets and past-year questions.
  • Tech These formative assessment items could be in the form of different SLS Question types. The corresponding AI-feedback assistants (e.g. ShortAnsFA, Annotated FA) could also be enabled.
  • Relate to NOS through reflection prompts
    • “How does this learning experience reveal about how scientific knowledge is built, tested and revised?”
  • Tech In an Elective Module, this question could be posed as a Free Response Question, with a suggested answer.
  • Explain the discrepant event and identify further contexts themselves
Take ActionInquiring like scientists, students study, analyse, compare, and organise information that has been curated for them, identifying patterns, discerning the structure of a concept and identifying the instances when that new knowledge should be used.
Understand Practices of ScienceCBL supports W7 (using and developing models) and W8 (constructing explanations) as students organise knowledge hierarchically. It also supports N1-N2 (understanding scientific knowledge as model-building with order and consistency).

Relate

to students’ prior knowledge and lived experiences
Teachers' Role
  • Pose a challenge that draws on prior knowledge / lived experience
    • "How can you make a single sheet of paper fall at the same rate as a ream of paper?
  • Tech A ‘Discussion’ could be set up on SLS for students to make their thinking visible in-class. Teacher can then use ‘Data Assistant’ to consolidate their responses and generate a whole-class overview.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, the SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner, probing student reasoning using prompts like "What did you try? / Why did you think that would work? / What did you observe? / Why did you think your method worked / not work?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students in-class.
Students' Role
  • Generate ideas and investigate using the sheet of paper and ream of paper provided.
AppreciateWhen real-world contexts are used to illustrate concepts, students can see the relevance of science.

Acquire

knowledge of single concepts through meaning making
Teachers' Role
  • Sequence cases and phenomena
    • "What do you predict will happen when we drop a bowling ball and a feather at the same time?"
    • "Now what do you predict will happen in a vacuum?"
  • Tech To make students’ thinking visible, a ‘Discussion’ could sequence the bowling ball and feather videos with prediction prompts between each: "Predict, then observe. What surprised you?" Teacher can then use ‘Data Assistant’ to get a consolidated in-class overview.
  • Tech An HTML5 interactive could allow students to vary mass and surface area and observe the effect on fall rate.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, the SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner using prompts like "What pattern did you notice in the interactive? / Does this match what you saw in the video? / What concept might explain this?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students.
Students' Role
  • Predict, observe, and make meaning
AppreciateWhen real-world contexts are used to illustrate concepts, students can see the relevance of science.

Connect

different concepts
Teachers' Role
  • (Other related concepts (e.g. weight, free body diagram, acceleration, net force) taught in previous lessons.)
  • Provide the context
    • "Why does the skydiver change his posture during the rescue?"
    • "Can you use a free body diagram to show what is happening to the forces?"
    • "What would help explain motion?"
  • Tech A ‘Discussion’ presents the skydiving video with minimal framing: "Why does the skydiver change posture? Use the interactive if it helps.”
  • Tech A dynamically-linked HTML5 interactive showing multiple representations could be made available but students decide how to use it. Teacher could use the interactive in-class with the overview of responses generated by ‘Data Assistant’.
  • Tech In an Elective Module, SLS chatbot is configured as a Socratic questioner, probing the student's emerging generalisation without confirming it: “What could help explain motion? "You wrote that more air resistance slows the skydiver down. Is that always true?" "What does your free body diagram show about the net force when air resistance equals weight?" "How would you refine your statement to account for that?" The chatbot could also be activated in-class, within the ‘Live Chat’ for each small group of students.
Students' Role
  • Generate the relationship question and construct the generalisation with facilitation
Understand Disciplinary IdeasCBL is explicitly designed to develop deep conceptual understanding of Disciplinary Ideas that transcend context. The process of connecting isolated facts to big ideas cultivates curiosity and open-mindedness.

Transfer

learning to novel contexts and the real world
Teachers' Role
  • Provide the transfer context
    • "What do you notice when the parachute opens?" (skydiver seen to fly upwards)
    • "Can you explain this in your own words using what you have established?"
    • "Where else in the real world might your generalisation apply?"
  • Tech Students could individually replay the video embedded on SLS to observe the motion of the skydiver when the parachute is displayed.
  • Tech Students could also use the above-mentioned HTML5 interactive to evaluate their transfer.
  • Tech SLS chatbot could be configured as a Socratic questioner, probing the student's explanation and adaptively introducing a further transfer context based on the quality of the response: "What do you notice when the parachute opens? Explain this in your own words using your generalisation. / You said the skydiver moves upwards. Does your generalisation predict this? Walk me through it. / Your explanation holds here. Can you think of another real-world situation where the same generalisation applies?"
  • Provide additional contexts through worksheets and past-year questions.
  • Tech These formative assessment items could be in the form of different SLS Question types. The corresponding AI-feedback assistants (e.g. ShortAnsFA, Annotated FA) could also be enabled.
  • Relate to NOS through reflection prompts
    • “How does this learning experience reveal about how scientific knowledge is built, tested and revised?”
  • Tech In an Elective Module, this question could be posed as a Free Response Question, with a suggested answer.
Students' Role
  • Explain the discrepant event and identify further contexts themselves
Take ActionInquiring like scientists, students study, analyse, compare, and organise information that has been curated for them, identifying patterns, discerning the structure of a concept and identifying the instances when that new knowledge should be used.
Understand Practices of ScienceCBL supports W7 (using and developing models) and W8 (constructing explanations) as students organise knowledge hierarchically. It also supports N1-N2 (understanding scientific knowledge as model-building with order and consistency).
  • The above learning experience can be differentiated for different student profiles as follows. The essence of CBL is preserved in all variations – students construct the generalisations.
  • Within the same class, there could be students of different readiness, and teachers can provide differentiated instruction at different phases.
StageRoleFor lower readiness studentsFor higher readiness students
RelateTeacher
  • activates prior knowledge through targeted questions
  • presents a puzzling phenomenon with no framing
Students
  • respond within a teacher-defined frame.
  • make their own connections to prior knowledge.
AcquireTeacher
  • controls case selection: examples and non-examples
  • presents raw, unsorted cases;
Students
  • identify shared attributes and name the concept.
  • sort, name, and explain the concept independently.
ConnectTeacher
  • provides the relationship question
  • provides a minimal prompt
Students
  • construct the generalisation in response
  • identify the relevant concepts, generate the relationship question, and construct the generalisation independently.
TransferTeacher
  • provides the transfer context and directs students to apply their generalisation to it
  • provides minimal prompt
Students
  • apply generalisation to the transfer context
  • students identify their own transfer context, apply the generalisation, and evaluate its limits.
Download SSI.docx
  • Socio-Scientific Issue-based Learning (SSIBL) is a pedagogical approach where students learn science through engaging with authentic, open-ended and socially relevant issues that are closely connected to scientific knowledge. These issues often involve ethical, social, economic, environmental or political considerations, and usually do not have a single correct answer. 
  • SSIBL is especially useful when the issue: 
  • is authentic and meaningful to students
  • is connected to scientific concepts, evidence and practices
  • involves ethical, societal or environmental implications
  • requires students to consider multiple perspectives and trade-offs
  • allows for more than one defensible position
  • supports students in making informed decisions and taking responsible action
  • SSIBL helps students move beyond learning science as a body of knowledge. It enables them to apply scientific understanding to real-world issues, evaluate evidence, reason through trade-offs, and justify decisions using both scientific and ethical considerations. 
  • A typical SSIBL learning experience involves three connected phases:
  • Encountering a Focal Issue
  • Students encounter a meaningful socio-scientific issue and develop awareness of its scientific and societal dimensions.
  • Engaging with key Ideas and Practices
  • Students develop and apply Disciplinary Core Ideas, Scientific Practices and Crosscutting Concepts while engaging in socio-scientific reasoning. This includes considering evidence, stakeholders, perspectives, values, uncertainty and the limitations of science.
  • Synthesising Key Ideas and Practices
  • Students draw together their scientific understanding and reasoning to develop, communicate and justify a position, recommendation, policy, solution or course of action.

Adapted from: Sadler, T.D., Foulk, J.A, & Friedrichsen, P.J. (2017). Evolution of a model for socio-scientific issue teaching and learning. International Journal of Education in Mathematics, Science and Technology, 5(2), 75-87

  • Across the learning sequence, students should also have opportunities to evaluate the reliability of information sources and reflect on or refine their own beliefs and positions regarding the issue.
  • The following questions are characteristic of SSIBL: 
  • What is the socio-scientific issue we are examining?
  • What scientific knowledge is needed to understand the issue?
  • What evidence is available, and how reliable is it?
  • Who are the stakeholders affected by this issue?
  • What ethical principles or values are involved?
  • What different perspectives or trade-offs need to be considered?
  • What decision or course of action can be justified using science, evidence and ethical reasoning?
  • In the context of science education, SSIBL supports students in developing scientific literacy by helping them understand how science interacts with society. It also provides opportunities to develop the Practices of Science, especially in using evidence, evaluating claims, constructing arguments and making informed decisions.
  • This LE is situated within a lesson which includes all components of the SSIBL framework. (Note: Not all lessons need to have all components)
  • SSI Ethical Dilemma: Should vaccination against Disease X be compulsory in Singapore?
  • Disease X is a novel disease. It is highly contagious and can lead to serious respiratory problems or even death, especially in people who have weaker immune systems. Many patients would need to be hospitalised as they will require respiratory support. The vaccine for Disease X has been recently developed and can be used for ages 12 years and above.
  • You will now take on the roles of lawmakers in Singapore and imagine that you are in the situation where Singapore has many Disease X infections. The vaccine against Disease X has just been developed and approved for use by the World Health Organization.
StageTeacher's RoleStudents' RoleAlignment of SSIBL to the 3 aims
Encountering a Focal Issue
  • Teacher presents the SSI – Should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory, to provide context to the lesson. For students to make a decision as lawmakers, they need to learn more about vaccination in Singapore first.
  • The SSI is presented first to build instruction around a compelling issue that connects to both science ideas and societal concerns
  • Tech Teacher shows students Interactive or Excel Immunisation chart against various diseases based on age in Singapore.
  • Teacher prompts students to generate questions and from conclusions from the chart.
  • Teacher prompts students to think why vaccines against diphtheria and measles are compulsory in Singapore.
  • Teacher introduces students to the Singapore Infectious Disease Act.
  • Students generate questions and form conclusions from the chart.
  • Students generate reasons as to why vaccinations against diphtheria and measles are compulsory in Singapore, but other vaccines are recommended.
  • In doing so, students begin to develop awareness of the ways in which science ideas have bearing on the issue, as well as the social concerns that emerge from it
  • Tech Students can interact with AI-Chatbot to learn more about the Singapore Infectious Disease Act as well as the diseases featured in the chart.
  • Tech Padlet, SLS DAT or Classpoint can be used to collate and summarise students’ responses.
UnderstandStudents' prior knowledge is activated Students are engaged in formulating conclusions and explanations from data, mirroring authentic scientific practices.
AppreciateStudents appreciate vaccination through their lived experience of the COVID-19 pandemic, providing a meaningful entry point for appreciating how scientific knowledge shapes public health decisions.
Engaging with Key Ideas and Practices
  • Teacher groups students to carry out Jigsaw learning to learn more about vaccination and their impacts.
  • Tech Teacher supports students in using digital tools to consolidate learning and providing different media resources to support the different learning preferences of students.
  • Teacher gets students to form individual positions on the SSI – Should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory in Singapore?
  • Tech Teacher can use tools such as Mentimeter or Classpoint to provide visualisation of the class responses.
  • Teacher introduces the four bioethical principles that serve as a guide for making reasoned judgements.
    • Respect for Persons
    • Justice
    • Minimise Harm
    • Maximise Benefits
  • These principles support socio-scientific reasoning by helping students account for the complexity of the socio-scientific issues and analyse it from multiple perspectives.
  • Teacher guides students to classify the reasons under each bioethical principle.
  • Teacher facilitates whole-class discussion on categorising the reasons under the principles, as well as highlighting reasons that do not fall under any of the principles.
  • Tech Teacher may use AI-supported tools to summarise discussion board responses, identify common patterns, or surface contrasting explanations for discussion.
  • Teacher also scaffolds students to employ skepticism when analysing information sources and to consider dimensions of the issue that remain subject to ongoing scientific inquiry.
  • Students generate reasons to support their position.
  • Tech Tools such as Mentimeter or Classpoint can be used to collate and summarise students’ responses.
  • Students obtain a duplicate copy of the class reasons for and against vaccination against Disease X. Students read through responses and classify them under the 4 bioethical principles.
  • This mirrors the Socio-scientific reasoning practice of analysing issues from multiple perspectives and exploring the social, political, and ethical dimensions that make the issue difficult to resolve. 
  • Tech Students can interact with AI Chatbot to clarify their understanding of each bioethical principle. AI Chatbot may provide contextual examples of what each principle means.
  • Tech Students can use AI tools like Snork , Transcribe, Vocaroo to record their discussion and to summarise their discussion for presentation. Students can use Notebook LM or ChatGPT to generate alternative presentation representations of their discussion.
  • When using media resources, students are encouraged to consider the source, purpose, potential biases, of the information they encounter.
  • Throughout this phase, students also have opportunities to reflect on and refine their own beliefs and perspectives on the issue, rather than reserving this only for the culminating activity.
UnderstandStudents engage in evidence-based reasoning, data collection, and analysis which are core science practices that deepen understanding of disciplinary concepts. As students communicate findings and engage in argumentation, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence. They also gain greater understanding about decision-making of SSIs based on bioethical principles. Students also develop understanding by considering the nature and limitations of scientific evidence in the context of the vaccination issue.
AppreciateStudents appreciate different perspectives about the SSI whilst interacting with media resources, learning that scientific knowledge is represented and interpreted differently by various stakeholders.
Take ActionSome student agency is involved as they take ownership of their own and the group's learning of the issue. Through collaborative learning, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings. The use of multimedia resources encourages students to persist through uncertainty, question information sources and biases and develop the curiosity and resilience needed for lifelong scientific inquiry. Through collaborative investigations, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings. Through bioethical reasoning activity, students develop informed views and positions on the SSI of compulsory vaccination, building the capacity to engage in future discourse on other SSIs and broader STSE issues, and ultimately becoming scientifically literate citizens who can participate responsibly in society.
Synthesising Key Ideas and Practices
  • Teacher re-surfaces the SSI ethical dilemma to the class – as Singaporean lawmakers, should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory?
  • Teacher scaffolds students to craft a policy paper as lawmakers.
  • This culminating activity is designed to encourage students to synthesise the science ideas, science practices, and socio-scientific reasoning practices they have developed throughout the LE, and to reflect on how their own perspectives interact with these dimensions
  • Teacher provides feedback on the scientific validity and quality of students’ explanations and reasoning.
  • Tech Teacher use AI tools such as Snokrl, SLS SAFA/AFA to provide feedback to students’ responses.
  • Teacher concludes the lesson to explain that issue of making vaccination compulsory is complex and requires justification not only using scientific evidence but also using bioethical principles.
  • Teacher gets students to reflect on their understanding of vaccination and their views on vaccination.
  • Students communicate and justify their stand in the policy paper, providing a strong justification that includes a clear decision, confirmatory facts, reference to ethical principles, and consideration of alternative views and rebuttals.
  • Requirements of the policy paper include use of evidence to support recommendations, analysis of the problem and its impacts, and evaluation of multiple perspectives mirroring the dimensions of rigorous socio-scientific issue argumentation.
  • Tech Students write their policy paper collaboratively using online collaborative platforms (e.g., Google Document, Office 365 Word)
  • Tech Students use AI tools like ChatGPT, Grammarly to edit and refine the writing.
  • Tech Students can share their policy papers on collaborative platforms like SLS or Padlet for critique by classmates.
  • Tech Students submit their reflections via SLS or Padlet to form their digital portfolio.
UnderstandAs students communicate findings and engage in peer critique, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence.
AppreciateThrough collaboration and discussion, students move beyond knowledge and begin to see science as a meaningful driver of positive change. Students appreciate the power of scientific knowledge in shaping public health decisions
Take ActionThrough crafting and defending a policy paper, students apply their scientific understanding and ethical reasoning to take a justified position on a real-world issue. This prepares students not just with content knowledge, but with the capacity to consider, make decisions about, and engage in discourses on complex societal issues. In doing so, students develop their identities as informed, responsible contributors to public discourse on science-related societal issues.

Encountering a Focal Issue

Teacher's Role
  • Teacher presents the SSI – Should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory, to provide context to the lesson. For students to make a decision as lawmakers, they need to learn more about vaccination in Singapore first.
  • The SSI is presented first to build instruction around a compelling issue that connects to both science ideas and societal concerns
  • Tech Teacher shows students Interactive or Excel Immunisation chart against various diseases based on age in Singapore.
  • Teacher prompts students to generate questions and from conclusions from the chart.
  • Teacher prompts students to think why vaccines against diphtheria and measles are compulsory in Singapore.
  • Teacher introduces students to the Singapore Infectious Disease Act.
Students' Role
  • Students generate questions and form conclusions from the chart.
  • Students generate reasons as to why vaccinations against diphtheria and measles are compulsory in Singapore, but other vaccines are recommended.
  • In doing so, students begin to develop awareness of the ways in which science ideas have bearing on the issue, as well as the social concerns that emerge from it
  • Tech Students can interact with AI-Chatbot to learn more about the Singapore Infectious Disease Act as well as the diseases featured in the chart.
  • Tech Padlet, SLS DAT or Classpoint can be used to collate and summarise students’ responses.
UnderstandStudents' prior knowledge is activated Students are engaged in formulating conclusions and explanations from data, mirroring authentic scientific practices.
AppreciateStudents appreciate vaccination through their lived experience of the COVID-19 pandemic, providing a meaningful entry point for appreciating how scientific knowledge shapes public health decisions.

Engaging with Key Ideas and Practices

Teacher's Role
  • Teacher groups students to carry out Jigsaw learning to learn more about vaccination and their impacts.
  • Tech Teacher supports students in using digital tools to consolidate learning and providing different media resources to support the different learning preferences of students.
  • Teacher gets students to form individual positions on the SSI – Should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory in Singapore?
  • Tech Teacher can use tools such as Mentimeter or Classpoint to provide visualisation of the class responses.
  • Teacher introduces the four bioethical principles that serve as a guide for making reasoned judgements.
    • Respect for Persons
    • Justice
    • Minimise Harm
    • Maximise Benefits
  • These principles support socio-scientific reasoning by helping students account for the complexity of the socio-scientific issues and analyse it from multiple perspectives.
  • Teacher guides students to classify the reasons under each bioethical principle.
  • Teacher facilitates whole-class discussion on categorising the reasons under the principles, as well as highlighting reasons that do not fall under any of the principles.
  • Tech Teacher may use AI-supported tools to summarise discussion board responses, identify common patterns, or surface contrasting explanations for discussion.
  • Teacher also scaffolds students to employ skepticism when analysing information sources and to consider dimensions of the issue that remain subject to ongoing scientific inquiry.
Students' Role
  • Students generate reasons to support their position.
  • Tech Tools such as Mentimeter or Classpoint can be used to collate and summarise students’ responses.
  • Students obtain a duplicate copy of the class reasons for and against vaccination against Disease X. Students read through responses and classify them under the 4 bioethical principles.
  • This mirrors the Socio-scientific reasoning practice of analysing issues from multiple perspectives and exploring the social, political, and ethical dimensions that make the issue difficult to resolve. 
  • Tech Students can interact with AI Chatbot to clarify their understanding of each bioethical principle. AI Chatbot may provide contextual examples of what each principle means.
  • Tech Students can use AI tools like Snork , Transcribe, Vocaroo to record their discussion and to summarise their discussion for presentation. Students can use Notebook LM or ChatGPT to generate alternative presentation representations of their discussion.
  • When using media resources, students are encouraged to consider the source, purpose, potential biases, of the information they encounter.
  • Throughout this phase, students also have opportunities to reflect on and refine their own beliefs and perspectives on the issue, rather than reserving this only for the culminating activity.
UnderstandStudents engage in evidence-based reasoning, data collection, and analysis which are core science practices that deepen understanding of disciplinary concepts. As students communicate findings and engage in argumentation, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence. They also gain greater understanding about decision-making of SSIs based on bioethical principles. Students also develop understanding by considering the nature and limitations of scientific evidence in the context of the vaccination issue.
AppreciateStudents appreciate different perspectives about the SSI whilst interacting with media resources, learning that scientific knowledge is represented and interpreted differently by various stakeholders.
Take ActionSome student agency is involved as they take ownership of their own and the group's learning of the issue. Through collaborative learning, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings. The use of multimedia resources encourages students to persist through uncertainty, question information sources and biases and develop the curiosity and resilience needed for lifelong scientific inquiry. Through collaborative investigations, students develop strong communication and collaboration skills as they work effectively in teams to co-construct knowledge and share findings. Through bioethical reasoning activity, students develop informed views and positions on the SSI of compulsory vaccination, building the capacity to engage in future discourse on other SSIs and broader STSE issues, and ultimately becoming scientifically literate citizens who can participate responsibly in society.

Synthesising Key Ideas and Practices

Teacher's Role
  • Teacher re-surfaces the SSI ethical dilemma to the class – as Singaporean lawmakers, should vaccination against Disease X be made compulsory?
  • Teacher scaffolds students to craft a policy paper as lawmakers.
  • This culminating activity is designed to encourage students to synthesise the science ideas, science practices, and socio-scientific reasoning practices they have developed throughout the LE, and to reflect on how their own perspectives interact with these dimensions
  • Teacher provides feedback on the scientific validity and quality of students’ explanations and reasoning.
  • Tech Teacher use AI tools such as Snokrl, SLS SAFA/AFA to provide feedback to students’ responses.
  • Teacher concludes the lesson to explain that issue of making vaccination compulsory is complex and requires justification not only using scientific evidence but also using bioethical principles.
  • Teacher gets students to reflect on their understanding of vaccination and their views on vaccination.
Students' Role
  • Students communicate and justify their stand in the policy paper, providing a strong justification that includes a clear decision, confirmatory facts, reference to ethical principles, and consideration of alternative views and rebuttals.
  • Requirements of the policy paper include use of evidence to support recommendations, analysis of the problem and its impacts, and evaluation of multiple perspectives mirroring the dimensions of rigorous socio-scientific issue argumentation.
  • Tech Students write their policy paper collaboratively using online collaborative platforms (e.g., Google Document, Office 365 Word)
  • Tech Students use AI tools like ChatGPT, Grammarly to edit and refine the writing.
  • Tech Students can share their policy papers on collaborative platforms like SLS or Padlet for critique by classmates.
  • Tech Students submit their reflections via SLS or Padlet to form their digital portfolio.
UnderstandAs students communicate findings and engage in peer critique, they develop values such as objectivity, integrity, and respect for evidence.
AppreciateThrough collaboration and discussion, students move beyond knowledge and begin to see science as a meaningful driver of positive change. Students appreciate the power of scientific knowledge in shaping public health decisions
Take ActionThrough crafting and defending a policy paper, students apply their scientific understanding and ethical reasoning to take a justified position on a real-world issue. This prepares students not just with content knowledge, but with the capacity to consider, make decisions about, and engage in discourses on complex societal issues. In doing so, students develop their identities as informed, responsible contributors to public discourse on science-related societal issues.
  • The above LE can be differentiated based on student readiness.
  • The essence of SSIBL is preserved in the differentiation, where students will engage with authentic socio-scientific issues, apply their scientific understanding, explore multiple perspectives and engage in ethical reasoning to make informed decisions as a scientifically literate citizen.
StageFor lower readiness studentsFor higher readiness students
Encountering a Focal Issue
  • Present the SSI through a familiar and concrete context to make the issue accessible
  • Break down the focal issue into smaller, explicit sub-questions to help students understand the problem
  • Pre-teach key vocabulary and scientific concepts needed to engage with the issue
  • Provide simplified or selected data sets for students to interpret
  • Scaffold data interpretation using guiding questions, sentence starters and key helping words to support students in formulating conclusions
  • Provide partially completed charts, tables or summaries to help students identify patterns and draw initial conclusions
  • Present a more complex version of the focal issue, including uncertainty, competing interests or real-world constraints.
  • Provide larger or more varied data sets for students to analyse independently.
  • Task students to compare across different sources or sets of data to generate questions, identify patterns and formulate evidence-based conclusions.
  • Encourage students to examine how different stakeholders may frame or interpret the SSI differently.
  • Prompt students to consider how scientific, social, economic and ethical perspectives influence the discussion around the issue.
Engaging with Key Ideas and Practices
  • Curate a limited set of reliable information sources for students to work with.
  • Provide guiding questions to help students extract relevant scientific ideas and evidence from the sources.
  • Use explicit prompts to support students in evaluating the credibility and relevance of information sources.
  • Provide templates for organising and presenting data, such as claim-evidence-reasoning tables or comparison charts.
  • Model how to generate reasons before asking students to develop their own justifications.
  • Introduce one clear ethical framework, such as the four bioethical principles, and model how it can be applied to a sample claim.
  • Allow students greater autonomy to extend their inquiry beyond teacher-provided sources to include credible online sources.
  • Encourage students to examine the SSI from scientific, social, economic, political and ethical viewpoints.
  • Introduce conflicting or alternative viewpoints so that students can evaluate evidence and engage in argumentation.
  • Guide students to compare different ethical frameworks, such as Principlism, Utilitarianism, Deontology, Virtue Ethics and the Ethics of Care.
  • Invite students to decide which ethical principles are most relevant to the issue and justify their choices.
  • Ask students to independently sort, classify and prioritise their reasons, while explaining the basis for their decisions.
Synthesising Key Ideas and Practices
  • Focus the task on helping students take a personal stand supported by scientific evidence and simple ethical reasoning.
  • Provide a structured template for students to organise their final response, such as claim, evidence, reasoning and recommendation.
  • Use guiding questions to help students craft a simple policy recommendation or position statement.
  • Facilitate guided reflection on how students’ understanding of vaccination and their initial views may have changed.
  • Allow students to communicate their final position through differentiated modes, such as a short-written response, infographic, recorded explanation or structured oral presentation.
  • Engage students in a more authentic decision-making task, such as a mock parliamentary debate on compulsory vaccination.
  • Require students to consider how scientific evidence, ethical reasoning and societal values inform real-world policy decisions.
  • Ask students to investigate vaccination policies in other countries and compare their effectiveness based on social, economic and political factors.
  • Require students to respond explicitly to counterclaims, critique and new evidence before refining their recommendations.
  • Encourage students to propose feasible actions for different stakeholders, such as individuals, schools, healthcare providers, government agencies or international organisations.
  • Guide students to justify their final policy position with a balanced consideration of evidence, values, trade-offs and consequences.
Reference
  • Sadler, T.D., Foulk, J.A, & Friedrichsen, P.J. (2017). Evolution of a model for socio-scientific issue teaching and learning. International Journal of Education in Mathematics, Science and Technology, 5(2), 75-87.
